source_paper_title: Lipschitz Stability of Least-Squares Problems Regularized by Functions with C2-Cone Reducible Conjugates

context:
Page 19 (Conclusion), paragraph beginning 'One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future...'; also reiterated on page 20.

One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. ... Finding the Lipschitz modulus without relying on second-order information of \(g\), as in our approach, remains an open area of research that we wish to continue exploring.
title: Compute Lipschitz modulus of regularized least-squares solution mapping under stability
background_latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces, \(A\in \mathcal{L}(X,Y)\) a linear operator with adjoint \(A^*\), \(b\in Y\), and \(\mu>0\). Let \(g:X\to \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed, proper, and convex. Consider the regularized least-squares problem
\[
\min_{x\in X}\; F_{A,b,\mu}(x):=\frac{1}{2\mu}\|Ax-b\|^2+g(x),
\]
with solution mapping
\[
S(A,b,\mu):=\operatorname{argmin}_{x\in X} F_{A,b,\mu}(x).
\]
Assume that around a reference parameter triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) the mapping \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz, i.e., there exist neighborhoods \(\mathcal{U}\) of \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) and a constant \(L\ge 0\) such that
\[
\|S(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-S(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\le L\,\|(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\quad \forall (A_i,b_i,\mu_i)\in \mathcal{U}.
\]
The infimum of all such \(L\) (for a fixed choice of local neighborhoods) is called the (local) Lipschitz modulus of \(S\) at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\); informally, it is the smallest constant governing the local Lipschitz bound.

The paper establishes first-order characterizations for when this Lipschitz stability holds, but notes that computing the Lipschitz modulus in general can be approached via coderivatives (which typically require second-order-type variational information about \(g\)).
problem_definition_latex: Determine (or provide computable formulas/estimates for) the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S\) at a reference triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) in the regime where \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz around \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\), using an approach that does not rely on involved second-order variational structures of the regularizer \(g\) (e.g., avoiding explicit computation of second-order objects such as coderivatives of \(\partial g\) that are difficult beyond polyhedral cases).
latex:

\[

\[
S(A,b,\mu):=\operatorname{argmin}_{x\in X} F_{A,b,\mu}(x).
\]
Assume that around a reference parameter triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) the mapping \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz, i.e., there exist neighborhoods \(\mathcal{U}\) of \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) and a constant \(L\ge 0\) such that
\[
\|S(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-S(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\le L\,\|(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\quad \forall (A_i,b_i,\mu_i)\in \mathcal{U}.

The paper establishes first-order characterizations for when this Lipschitz stability holds, but notes that computing the Lipschitz modulus in general can be approached via coderivatives (which typically require second-order-type variational information about \(g\)).

Determine (or provide computable formulas/estimates for) the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S\) at a reference triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) in the regime where \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz around \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\), using an approach that does not rely on involved second-order variational structures of the regularizer \(g\) (e.g., avoiding explicit computation of second-order objects such as coderivatives of \(\partial g\) that are difficult beyond polyhedral cases).
location_in_paper: Page 19 (Conclusion), paragraph beginning 'One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future...'; also reiterated on page 20.
open_problem_quote: One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. ... Finding the Lipschitz modulus without relying on second-order information of \(g\), as in our approach, remains an open area of research that we wish to continue exploring.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The task is to derive general formulas or computable bounds for a modulus, not to refute a universal claim. While one can test candidate bounds on instances, disproving or validating a proposed general expression would still require analytic arguments beyond finite counterexample search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could propose and empirically evaluate candidate upper/lower bounds or estimators of the modulus on families of computable instances (e.g., specific choices of \(g\) and random \(A,b\)) by numerically estimating local Lipschitz constants. However, translating empirical patterns into general, provable formulas is nontrivial, so computational search is mainly useful for conjecturing sharp constants or discovering structure in special classes.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
